results.possibleIdentity: Jerry Spivack was an engineer and research scientist at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs) in Murray Hill, New Jersey, primarily active during the 1960s and 1970s. He was a pioneer in the field of interactive computer graphics and was a significant participant in the intersection of art and technology. In 1968, his interactive computer art piece titled "Computer Descending a Staircase" (a pun on Marcel Duchamp's "Nude Descending a Staircase") was selected by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) for the landmark exhibition "Some More Beginnings" at the Brooklyn Museum. The piece utilized slides created by a microfilm plotter, allowing viewers to manipulate knobs to control the intensity of RGB color filters over random grayscale squares, effectively turning the observer into an artist. Spivack's technical work at Bell Labs spanned communications research, computer research, and visual perception research, often collaborating with other pioneers like A. Michael Noll and Ken Knowlton. In his later career, he remained active in the legacy of E.A.T., serving as a Vice President of the organization and participating in historical panels regarding the origins of multimedia art. He was also associated with Columbia University's University Seminars, specifically those focused on "Ecology and Culture" and "Knowledge, Technology, and Social Systems."
